150.play: Duke Frederick, hearing how that every day

151.play: Men of great worth resorted to this forest,

152.play: Address'd a mighty power; which were on foot,

153.play: In his own conduct, purposely to take

154.play: His brother here and put him to the sword:

155.play: And to the skirts of this wild wood he came;

156.play: Where meeting with an old religious man,

157.play: After some question with him, was converted

158.play: Both from his enterprise and from the world,

159.play: His crown bequeathing to his banish'd brother,

160.play: And all their lands restored to them again

161.play: That were with him exiled. This to be true,

164.play: Thou offer'st fairly to thy brothers' wedding:

165.play: To one his lands withheld, and to the other

166.play: A land itself at large, a potent dukedom.

167.play: First, in this forest, let us do those ends

168.play: That here were well begun and well begot:

169.play: And after, every of this happy number

170.play: That have endured shrewd days and nights with us

171.play: Shall share the good of our returned fortune,

172.play: According to the measure of their states.

174.play: And fall into our rustic revelry.

175.play: Play, music! And you, brides and bridegrooms all,

176.play: With measure heap'd in joy, to the measures fall.

177.play: JAQUES: Sir, by your patience. If I heard you rightly,

178.play: The duke hath put on a religious life

179.play: And thrown into neglect the pompous court?

181.play: JAQUES: To him will I : out of these convertites

182.play: There is much matter to be heard and learn'd.

183.play: You to your former honour I bequeath;

184.play: Your patience and your virtue well deserves it:

185.play: You to a love that your true faith doth merit:

186.play: You to your land and love and great allies:

187.play: You to a long and well-deserved bed:

188.play: And you to wrangling; for thy loving voyage

189.play: Is but for two months victuall'd. So, to your pleasures:

190.play: I am for other than for dancing measures.

192.play: JAQUES: To see no pastime I	what you would have

193.play: I'll stay to know at your abandon'd cave.

194.play: DUKE SENIOR: Proceed, proceed: we will begin these rites,

195.play: As we do trust they'll end, in true delights.

197.play: ROSALIND: It is not the fashion to see the lady the epilogue;

198.play: but it is no more unhandsome than to see the lord

199.play: the prologue. If it be true that good wine needs

200.play: no bush, 'tis true that a good play needs no

201.play: epilogue; yet to good wine they do use good bushes,

202.play: and good plays prove the better by the help of good

203.play: epilogues. What a case am I in then, that am

204.play: neither a good epilogue nor cannot insinuate with

205.play: you in the behalf of a good play! I am not

206.play: furnished like a beggar, therefore to beg will not

207.play: become me: my way is to conjure you; and I'll begin

208.play: with the women. I charge you, O women, for the love

209.play: you bear to men, to like as much of this play as

210.play: please you: and I charge you, O men, for the love

211.play: you bear to women--as I perceive by your simpering,

212.play: none of you hates them--that between you and the

213.play: women the play may please. If I were a woman I

214.play: would kiss as many of you as had beards that pleased

215.play: me, complexions that liked me and breaths that I

216.play: defied not: and, I am sure, as many as have good

217.play: beards or good faces or sweet breaths will, for my

218.play: kind offer, when I make curtsy, bid me farewell.
